label: Santa Anna's March to San Antonio (1836)
notes: Santa Anna marched ~6,000 soldiers from San Luis Potosí north through the desert in winter to besiege the Alamo. The grueling march caused significant attrition before any battle.

label: Santa Anna's Pursuit — Runaway Scrape (1836)
notes: After the Alamo, Santa Anna's army pursued Houston's retreating Texan force and the fleeing civilian 'Runaway Scrape' east toward the Louisiana border, ending at his catastrophic defeat at San Jacinto.

label: Houston's Strategic Retreat to San Jacinto (1836)
notes: Sam Houston controversially retreated east for six weeks after the Alamo and Goliad, choosing his ground carefully. He turned at Lynch's Ferry and attacked Santa Anna's encamped army at San Jacinto on April 21, 1836.

label: General Urrea's Coastal Sweep (1836)
notes: General José Urrea's cavalry column swept northward along the Texas coast, defeating Texan detachments at San Patricio (Feb 27), Agua Dulce (Mar 2), Coleto Creek (Mar 19-20), and accepting Fannin's surrender before the Goliad Massacre.

label: Taylor's Advance to the Rio Grande (Mar 1846)
notes: General Taylor marched his 3,500-man Army of Occupation south from Corpus Christi to the north bank of the Rio Grande, establishing Fort Texas opposite Matamoros. Mexico viewed this as invasion of disputed territory.

label: Taylor's Campaign Route to Monterrey (1846)
notes: After securing the Rio Grande, Taylor advanced through Camargo and Cerralvo to besiege Monterrey in September 1846. This 200-mile march through northern Mexico defined the northern theater of the war.

label: Taylor's Advance to Buena Vista (1846–47)
notes: After Monterrey, US forces under General Worth captured Saltillo; Taylor established his final defensive position at Buena Vista where Santa Anna's army attacked in February 1847.

label: Santa Anna's Winter March to Buena Vista (Jan–Feb 1847)
notes: Santa Anna marched 20,000 soldiers north from San Luis Potosí in winter conditions to attack Taylor. The march was so punishing that perhaps 4,000 men deserted or died of exposure before reaching Buena Vista.

label: Kearny's Army of the West — Santa Fe Trail (Jun–Aug 1846)
notes: Kearny's 1,700-man Army of the West marched 900 miles down the Santa Fe Trail from Fort Leavenworth, Kansas to capture New Mexico without a battle. One of the most ambitious marches of the war.

label: Kearny's March to California (Sep–Dec 1846)
notes: After securing New Mexico, Kearny continued with 100 dragoons along the Gila River corridor through Arizona to San Diego, California. The march through desert terrain was extraordinarily difficult.

label: Doniphan's March to Chihuahua (Dec 1846–Mar 1847)
notes: Colonel Doniphan's 900 Missouri Volunteers marched south from Santa Fe through El Paso del Norte to Chihuahua City, fighting at Brazito (Dec 25) and Sacramento (Feb 28). Total march distance exceeded 2,000 miles.

label: Arista's Retreat After Resaca de la Palma (May 1846)
notes: After being routed at Resaca de la Palma, General Arista's army fled in disarray across the Rio Grande to Matamoros, abandoning artillery, supplies, and their wounded. The collapse of the Army of the North opened northern Mexico to US invasion.

label: Scott's Amphibious Landing at Veracruz (Mar 9, 1847)
notes: 12,000 US troops landed on the beaches south of Veracruz in surf boats — the largest US amphibious operation to date. 67 specially designed landing craft transported the entire army in a single afternoon.

label: Scott's March: Veracruz to Cerro Gordo (Apr 1847)
notes: Scott's army marched inland along the National Highway through the yellow fever zone below 3,000 feet elevation, racing to reach the mountain pass at Cerro Gordo before the vómito negro season killed his men.

label: Scott's March: Cerro Gordo to Puebla (Apr–May 1847)
notes: Following the rout at Cerro Gordo, Scott's army advanced through Jalapa and Perote to Puebla, which surrendered without resistance on May 15. Scott paused here three months to await reinforcements.

label: Scott's March: Puebla to Mexico City (Aug 1847)
notes: Scott boldly cut his supply line to Veracruz and advanced 75 miles to the Valley of Mexico — following Cortés's route. He crossed the mountains via the Chalco route to outflank Santa Anna's defenses south of the capital.

label: Santa Anna's Retreat After Cerro Gordo (Apr 1847)
notes: After the disaster at Cerro Gordo, Santa Anna fled on foot without his boots, abandoning his artillery, his personal coach and papers, and his artificial leg (later captured as a war trophy by Illinois troops).

label: Wool's Army of the Center March (Sep–Nov 1846)
notes: General John Wool marched his 3,000-man 'Army of the Center' from San Antonio through the Rio Grande and across the Coahuila desert to Saltillo, completing an 800-mile march without a significant battle.

label: Scott's Encirclement of Veracruz (Mar 9–29, 1847)
notes: US forces encircled Veracruz on land while the US Navy blockaded the port. Siege artillery was emplaced and the city bombarded over several days before Mexican Governor Morales surrendered on March 29.

label: Ampudia's Withdrawal from Monterrey (Sep 24, 1846)
notes: Under the controversial armistice terms granted by Taylor, General Ampudia's 7,303 soldiers withdrew from Monterrey to Saltillo with their weapons and artillery — allowing Mexico to reuse a beaten army, infuriating President Polk.

label: Frémont's Bear Flag Movement (Jun–Jul 1846)
notes: Captain John C. Frémont and his armed survey party encouraged and then joined American settlers in the Bear Flag revolt, moving from Monterey north to Sonoma. Frémont's irregular force claimed California for the United States before US naval forces arrived.

label: Stockton's California Pacification (Jul–Aug 1846)
notes: Commodore Stockton's Pacific Squadron landed marines along the California coast, occupying ports from San Francisco south to San Diego and Los Angeles, completing the initial US conquest of Alta California.

label: Kearny-Stockton Reconquest of Southern California (Jan 1847)
notes: After Kearny's battered army reached San Diego, combined US land and sea forces under Kearny and Stockton marched north to retake Los Angeles. Decisive battles at San Gabriel (Jan 8) and La Mesa (Jan 9) completed the reconquest.

label: Kearny's Gila River Route to California
notes: Kearny's 100 dragoons followed the Gila River west through present-day Arizona, guided by Kit Carson. This punishing desert route covered some 1,000 miles from Santa Fe to San Diego through largely unmapped terrain.

label: US Gulf Squadron Blockade Route (1846–47)
notes: The US Home Squadron under Commodore Conner blockaded Mexican Gulf ports from Tampico to Veracruz, seizing Tampico (Nov 1846) and providing the naval support essential for Scott's Veracruz landing in March 1847.

label: Scott's Flanking Approach to Contreras (Aug 1847)
notes: Scott's engineers found a route through the Pedregal lava field to outflank Santa Anna's defensive line south of Mexico City. The night march through treacherous volcanic rock set up the decisive 17-minute rout at Contreras.

label: Santa Anna Evacuates Mexico City (Sep 13–14, 1847)
notes: After Chapultepec fell, Santa Anna evacuated Mexico City during the night of September 13–14, 1847, retreating toward Puebla with remnants of his army. The capital was left to the city government to formally surrender to Scott.

label: Veracruz Beach Landing (Mar 9, 1847)
notes: US troops landed on the beach at Collado, south of Veracruz, in surf boats designed for the purpose. 67 landing craft delivered 12,000 troops in the afternoon without significant opposition — a landmark in US amphibious warfare.

label: Texas Annexation — Republic to US State (Dec 29, 1845)
notes: Texas formally transferred sovereignty from the Republic of Texas to the United States when President Polk signed the admission act. The boundary claim — Rio Grande vs. Nueces — was central to the subsequent war with Mexico.

label: Santa Anna's Retreat from Buena Vista (Feb 1847)
notes: Despite claiming victory in Mexican press, Santa Anna withdrew his mauled army south from Buena Vista, leaving Taylor in control of northern Mexico. The retreat exposed Mexico City to Scott's southern approach and ended the northern campaign.

label: Trist's Negotiations to Guadalupe Hidalgo (Dec 1847–Feb 1848)
notes: After defying his recall, Nicholas Trist negotiated with Mexican peace commissioners in secret. Meetings were held at the British legation and at Guadalupe Hidalgo. The final treaty was signed at the Basilica on February 2, 1848.
